FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Analyzes three consecutive waves (Years 2–4) of the U.S. ABCD Study (ABCD data release 6.0, https://doi.org/10.82525/jy7n-g441), approximately 7,260 participants across 21 U.S. sites.
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: ABCD Study (Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development), 21 U.S. sites, NIH-funded, 11,868 participants baseline age 9-10, NDA DOI: 10.82525/jy7n-g441, release 6.0 cited in Methods.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis: multilevel linear mixed-effects model (ADHD → VGAQ over time) plus parallel mediation model (ADHD → psychosocial mediators → VGAQ).
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis: multilevel linear regression (Years 2-4) plus parallel mediation model (Mplus 8.10) examining ADHD -> gaming addiction pathways, N~7,260-9,604.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Frontiers in Psychiatry (2026).
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Frontiers in Psychiatry, 17:1756782, published 06 March 2026; no indication of duplicate.
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific numeric estimate found anywhere in the paper. The primary outcome is VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), which measures gaming addiction symptoms for video games with platform unspecified. The predictors are ADHD diagnosis (KSADS-COMP) and psychosocial mediators (prosocial peer involvement, school involvement, family conflict, parental monitoring, etc.). No phone time, no social media variable, no named SM platform, no SMAQ/MPIQ appears anywhere in the main text, tables, or supplementary material. Step 2: No composite analyzed; exposure is VGAQ only. Under binding clarification, VGAQ (generic video game addiction questionnaire, unspecified platform) fails IC2 unless paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable, which is absent here.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate found anywhere in the paper. The exposure is ADHD diagnosis (KSADS-COMP); the primary outcome is VGAQ score (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire). Per binding clarification #2, VGAQ is a gaming variable and does not qualify as a phone/SM variable. Mediators are psychosocial variables (family conflict, school involvement, prosocial peer involvement, parental monitoring, prosocial behavior, school disengagement, school environment, rule breaking, parent behavior inventory) - none are phone/SM. No smartphone or social media variable appears in any main table, supplementary table, or sensitivity analysis. Step 2 not reached. Gaming-only study with no qualifying phone/SM variable.

title: Typologies of Family Functioning and 24-h Movement Behaviors.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Baseline cross-sectional data from the U.S. ABCD study, 21 research sites across the United States, 10,712 children aged 9-10 (NIMH Data DOI: 10.15154/1412097).
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Data from the ABCD study baseline; 10,712 children ages 9-10 recruited from 21 research sites across the United States. Explicitly identified as U.S. ABCD Study.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical analysis: latent profile analysis (LPA) to identify family typologies, followed by multilevel logistic regression examining associations between family typologies and number of 24-h movement behavior recommendations met.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical analysis: latent profile analysis (LPA) on family typologies followed by multilevel logistic regressions associating family profiles with meeting 24-h movement behavior guidelines. Numeric OR results reported.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Int. J. Environ. Res. Public Health; no indication of duplication.
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in IJERPH (2021); no indication of duplicate.
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific coefficient found anywhere in the paper. Screen time is measured via the 'Youth Screen Time Survey (12 items)' and analyzed only as a composite daily screen time score (weighted average across weekday and weekend). No breakdown by modality (phone, social media, texting, etc.) appears in any table or supplementary material. Step 2: The composite covers 'different types of media (e.g., watching shows or movies, texting)' per Section 2.3 — it explicitly includes watching shows/movies (non-phone/non-SM) alongside other media. The composite is not built exclusively from phone/SM/digital-socializing items. The outcome variable is meeting movement behavior guidelines (a composite of physical activity + screen time + sleep), not a phone/SM variable. NOT_MET under both steps.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable has its own coefficient anywhere in the paper. Screen time is analyzed only as part of a composite '24-h movement behavior' recommendation (meeting vs not meeting all three guidelines: PA + screen time + sleep combined), not as a standalone DV with its own estimate. Step 2: The screen time measure is the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey (12 items) described as assessing 'how much time children spend on different types of media (e.g., watching shows or movies, texting)' — a multi-activity composite that includes TV/movie-watching alongside texting, with no per-device or per-platform breakout anywhere in the paper. The composite fails Step 2 because it includes non-phone/non-SM items (shows or movies) bundled in. Discussion notes as a limitation that platform-specific data (e.g., Twitter, Instagram) were not examined, confirming no such analysis was conducted.

title: American Children's Screen Time: Diminished Returns of Household Income in Black Families.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Uses ABCD Study baseline data (2016, 21 sites, ages 9-11, n=15,022), Data Release 2.0 (NDA Study 634). ABCD data repository cited explicitly.
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, baseline 2016, 21 sites, ages 9-11; Curated Annual Release 2.0 (NDA Study 634).
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical analysis using mixed-effects regression models with DEAP software; reports regression coefficients, SEs, and p-values for family income predicting screen time.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical analysis: two mixed-effects regression models (Model 1 and Model 2 with interaction terms) predicting children's weekday screen time from household income and race on n=15,022 ABCD participants.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Information (Basel), 2020; no indication of duplicate or preprint of an included paper.
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Information (Basel) 2020; no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable has its own coefficient anywhere in the paper. Step 2: The screen time DV is a multi-device aggregate — Methods state parents reported time 'using different types of electronic devices (computer, cellphone, tablet, iPod, and other devices)' summed as a single weekday hours measure (screentime_ss_weekday). This bundles non-phone devices (computer, tablet, iPod) with cellphone without any per-device breakout. Fails Step 2 as multi-device aggregated time. Additionally, the only predictors analyzed are family income and race (demographics), so even if screen time qualified, the other-side would be demographic-only — not individual-trait-level.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Step 1: No numeric estimate exists where the variable label is phone/SM-specific (e.g., time on phone, time on SM, named platform). The outcome is 'screentime_ss_weekday', a parent-reported total weekday screen time variable that aggregates across computer, cell phone, tablet, iPod, and other devices without per-device breakout. Step 2: Composite includes non-phone/non-SM devices (computer, tablet, iPod, other). Additionally, the only independent variable on the other side is household income (demographic), with race, sex, marital status, age, and ethnicity as covariates — all demographic-only. Both conditions for NOT_MET are met: the composite is a multi-device aggregate (fails Step 2) and the other side is demographic-only.
